Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty inconsistent, but I tried to make it work with what I had going on. I'd usually set aside time on weekends and a couple of evenings during the week, aiming for maybe two hours at a stretch. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time with diagrams, charts, and color-coded notes rather than just reading through text. With 18 online courses to juggle, I had to be strategic about which ones got my attention each week. Some weeks I was more organized than others, depending on how anxious I was feeling about upcoming deadlines.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I'd try to set regular times, but honestly, when anxiety kicked in about an assignment, I'd end up cramming whenever I could. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying Tuesday and Thursday evenings, but then other weeks everything would shift because of work or just feeling overwhelmed. I learned to be flexible because rigid schedules just made me more stressed when I couldn't stick to them. Deadlines were really the main driver of when I'd put in serious hours.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements or if my peers had responded to something in the discussions. When I was feeling particularly anxious about my progress, I'd log in just to reassure myself I wasn't missing anything major. Honestly, the fear of falling behind was a big motivator for me to check in, even if I didn't always engage deeply once I was there.
Q4: It took a while, but eventually checking the LMS in the morning with my coffee became kind of automatic. I think it happened because I connected with a few classmates through the peer support system, and I wanted to see their messages and keep up with group discussions. Having those connections made logging in feel less like a chore and more like checking in with people. Once that social element was there, the habit formed more naturally. It wasn't perfect, but it became part of my routine most weeks.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really study the diagrams and visual explanations at my own pace. The discussion forums also became valuable once I got connected with peers who were going through the same struggles. Seeing how others approached problems helped me understand concepts I was stuck on. Anything with visual elements or real examples stuck with me way better than plain text readings.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped or delayed the longer reading materials, especially dense PDFs without any visual elements. They just didn't hold my attention, and with my anxiety levels, I'd get frustrated and give up if I wasn't understanding something quickly. Some of the optional practice quizzes I ignored because I was already stressed about the required ones. I also didn't engage much with some of the supplementary resources because I was already stretched thin across so many courses. I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If it had clear visual components or examples I could learn from, I was much more likely to engage with it. Knowing that my peers were also working on the same thing made it feel more worthwhile too, like we were in it together. I also valued activities where I could see immediate progress or get some kind of feedback. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford with everything else on my plate. Required ones had this weight of necessity that pushed me to complete them even when I was exhausted or anxious. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and then later never came because something required always took priority. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional things, but I had to make peace with not being able to do everything. The optional ones felt like they were designed for students with more time than I had.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download videos and PDFs to study offline, especially when I wanted to avoid distractions or when my internet was acting up. I made tons of handwritten notes with diagrams and color-coding because that's how I learn best. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to supplement what we were learning. My physical notebook was probably where most of my real learning happened, honestly.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out lecture slides and spend hours going over them with highlighters and sticky notes at my desk. I'd also study with peers in person or over calls, working through problems together without any of that showing in the system. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay it mentally while sketching out the concepts on paper. I did a lot of explaining concepts out loud to myself, which sounds weird but really helped me understand things. None of that deep processing ever registered anywhere digitally.
Q11: Yeah, this was actually my main approach for a lot of materials. I'd access something once to download it, then spend way more time with it offline than I ever did online. Videos especially, I'd download them and watch them multiple times on my laptop without being connected. It was easier to focus without the temptation to click around to other things online. My offline study time probably tripled what my online activity would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and everything felt impossible. When I had multiple deadlines converging across different courses, I'd basically freeze up and struggle to engage with anything productively. Family stuff came up a few times that pulled my attention away from school completely. During those times, even logging in felt overwhelming because I knew I was behind. I had to really lean on my resilience and remind myself that I'd gotten through tough times before.
Q13: All of those things affected me at different points. The workload across 18 courses was honestly brutal, and I had to constantly triage what got my attention. When I was fatigued, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about assignments made me avoid the LMS because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand. My other responsibilities meant I couldn't always engage during optimal times, so I'd end up studying when I was already drained.
Q14: Oh definitely, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious about other things to actually absorb what I was watching. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't really learning. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain wasn't present. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I didn't always have the energy to do better.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on things that felt too difficult or overwhelming. If an assignment seemed confusing, I'd put it off hoping it would make more sense later, which usually just made the anxiety worse. Sometimes I avoided discussion posts because I felt like I didn't have anything smart to contribute. The peer support helped with this because my classmates would encourage me to just start, even imperfectly. But yeah, avoidance was a real struggle for me, especially with high-stakes assignments.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be logged in way more frequently, watching videos, checking requirements, posting in discussions. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more. They created this urgency that cut through my tendency to procrastinate. But they also spiked my anxiety, so it was a double-edged sword. I'd often wish I'd started earlier, but the deadline pressure was what actually got me moving.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me when I actually got it. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more and made me feel like my effort was worth it. When feedback was delayed or minimal, I felt disconnected and unsure if I was on the right track. My peers giving me feedback through our support network was actually more consistent than instructor feedback sometimes. Good feedback made me want to log back in and keep trying; silence made me want to avoid the course.
Q18: The structure of having weekly modules helped me know what to focus on, even if I didn't always keep up. When things were clearly laid out with visual guides, I could plan my limited study time better. But when the structure was confusing or inconsistent, my anxiety would spike and I'd disengage. Having peer support built into the course structure was huge for me because it gave me people to lean on. The structure either helped me feel in control or completely overwhelmed, depending on how clear it was.
Q19: Visual elements and clear organization made engagement way easier for me. When I could see a roadmap of what was expected, I felt more capable of tackling it. Confusing navigation or walls of text made engagement harder because I'd get lost or frustrated. The discussion forums being easy to access helped because connecting with peers kept me accountable. Courses that assumed I had unlimited time or energy made it harder to stay engaged realistically.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying but struggling to produce work that meets the standard. They might be researching, watching videos, reading discussions, but then hit a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. Anxiety and perfectionism can make you spend hours preparing but then freeze when it's time to submit. Or maybe life circumstances keep interrupting their ability to finish what they start. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when someone is stressed.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my experience in some ways. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show in my activity logs. When I understood something, I didn't need to keep rewatching or rereading, so my activity might look low even though I'd mastered the material. Efficiency matters too, some students figure out what's essential and focus only on that. Low activity can mean someone found effective ways to learn outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active or how often you log in, which gives some insight into habits. They can track completion of specific tasks and time spent on different resources. But they miss all the offline learning, the mental processing, the conversations with peers outside the platform. They can't measure understanding or how much you're actually absorbing versus just clicking through. The emotional and cognitive side of learning is invisible in the data.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to things I already know. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to other people in the course, like we're learning together. It's showing up even when it's hard because I care about understanding, not just passing. Real engagement is active and intentional, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to keep going when things got tough. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I pushed through to complete assignments or the resilience it took to keep showing up with everything on my plate. My offline study habits, my handwritten notes full of diagrams, the times I explained concepts to myself out loud, none of that appears anywhere. It also wouldn't show how much my mental state affected my engagement day to day. The human struggle behind the data is completely invisible.
